summary:
Sulla's Dictatorship (82–79 BC) was Rome's first experience of one-man rule backed by a loyal army, established when Lucius Cornelius Sulla marched on Rome twice — in 88 and 82 BC — using his legions to seize power, the first Roman general to do so against his own city. Sulla's dictatorship was technically constitutional but in practice fundamentally transformed the relationship between military power and political legitimacy in the Republic.

Sulla had been a capable general in the [[event:1070|Social War]] and the [[event:1091|Mithridatic Wars]] before the political conflict between the optimates (senatorial traditionalists) and populares (reform faction, led by Gaius Marius) plunged Rome into civil war. His march on Rome in 88 BC — the first time a Roman general had led legions against the city — was provoked by his rival Marius stripping him of command of the eastern campaign. After his campaign against Mithridates, Sulla returned to Italy, defeated the populares in battle, and became dictator in 82 BC.

Sulla's dictatorship featured proscriptions — public lists of political enemies whose property was confiscated and who could be killed by anyone — that eliminated thousands of opponents and enriched Sulla's supporters. He then enacted a sweeping program of reforms strengthening the Senate and restricting the tribunes, apparently believing he could restore the Republic to stable senatorial governance. In 79 BC, he voluntarily resigned the dictatorship and retired, dying the following year. His attempt to fix the Republic's problems by force while preserving its forms was ultimately unsuccessful, but his march on Rome established a model that Julius Caesar and others would follow.
